cashier_1960_single: The golden age was real — if you were buying, not scanning. A cashier could actually own a home in most cities.
cashier_1960_dual: A little extra income and suddenly the map turns green. 1960 was genuinely that simple.
cashier_1980_single: The mortgage rate hit like a freight train. A cashier's salary didn't stand a chance in most cities.
cashier_1980_dual: A partner's income helped — but not enough. Half the map still said no.
cashier_2000_single: Not a collapse, just a slow squeeze. A cashier in 2000 was already locked out of the coasts.
cashier_2000_dual: Two incomes, and you could maybe make it work in Houston. Maybe.
cashier_2020_single: The crisis gets all the attention. But for a cashier, 2020 just confirmed what 1980 started.
cashier_2020_dual: A partner's paycheck moves the needle slightly. The map is still mostly red.
janitor_1960_single: 1960 rewarded the unglamorous. A janitor could buy a home — and that was just normal.
janitor_1960_dual: Two modest incomes in 1960 was practically a superpower. Green across the board.
janitor_1980_single: The rate spike didn't care about your work ethic. A janitor's dream deferred, coast to coast.
janitor_1980_dual: A second income softened the blow. Still a stretch in most places, but not impossible.
janitor_2000_single: By 2000, a janitor and a median home were strangers in most American cities.
janitor_2000_dual: Two salaries, one shot — mostly in the middle of the country.
janitor_2020_single: Nothing dramatic changed. It was already broken. 2020 just made it visible.
janitor_2020_dual: A partner's 40% helps — but this is still a red map story.
bus_driver_1960_single: Bus drivers built the suburbs they couldn't afford today. In 1960, they could buy there too.
bus_driver_1960_dual: A union job and a working partner in 1960? Comfortable almost everywhere.
bus_driver_1980_single: The rate shock hit working-class stability hard. Bus drivers felt it first and longest.
bus_driver_1980_dual: Extra income helped in cheaper cities. The coasts were already a different country.
bus_driver_2000_single: A solid job, a broken market. Bus drivers were priced out of the cities they served.
bus_driver_2000_dual: Two incomes opened a few more doors — mostly in Houston and Phoenix.
bus_driver_2020_single: Essential worker, unaffordable city. The irony writes itself.
bus_driver_2020_dual: A partner's income keeps a few cities reachable. The coasts remain a fantasy.
electrician_1960_single: A skilled trade in 1960 meant a house, a yard, and change to spare. The math worked.
electrician_1960_dual: Comfortable everywhere. This is the era the boomers remember — and they're not entirely wrong.
electrician_1980_single: Even skilled trades got burned by the rate spike. Electricians stretched thin across the map.
electrician_1980_dual: Two incomes rescued the picture in most cities. Still a rough decade either way.
electrician_2000_single: Better than average — but the coasts had already moved to a different income bracket.
electrician_2000_dual: Solidly middle of the map. A good decade to be a dual-income skilled household.
electrician_2020_single: Trades held their ground better than most. Still locked out of SF, but not everywhere.
electrician_2020_dual: One of the better stories in 2020. A partner's income tips most cities into comfortable.
teacher_1960_single: America valued teachers in 1960 — at least enough to let them buy a house.
teacher_1960_dual: Two teacher salaries in 1960 was practically a golden ticket. Green map, no contest.
teacher_1980_single: The rate crisis landed hard on public sector salaries. Teachers stretched thin.
teacher_1980_dual: A second income made it work in most cities — the midwest held up, the coasts didn't.
teacher_2000_single: Good at their job, bad timing on the market. Teachers started losing cities fast.
teacher_2000_dual: Better together — but San Francisco didn't care about your combined income.
teacher_2020_single: The teacher housing crisis is real. Most coastal cities are simply out of reach.
teacher_2020_dual: A partner's income reopens some cities. The map is still half orange.
nurse_1960_single: Nurses were undervalued in 1960 — but the housing market hadn't noticed yet.
nurse_1960_dual: A comfortable life almost everywhere. The good old days had some genuine good in them.
nurse_1980_single: Night shifts didn't cover the mortgage rate. Nurses stretched from coast to coast.
nurse_1980_dual: Extra income helped most cities land in stretch territory. Not great, not catastrophic.
nurse_2000_single: Growing wages, growing prices. Nurses ran to stand still in most markets.
nurse_2000_dual: Two incomes and a sensible city? Comfortable. San Francisco laughs either way.
nurse_2020_single: Nurses got a raise. Housing got a bigger one. Most coastal cities still out of reach.
nurse_2020_dual: A partner's income tips the balance in most cities. SF remains unimpressed.
developer_1960_single: Software developers existed. The housing market hadn't yet decided to punish them for it.
developer_1960_dual: Comfortable everywhere — though in 1960 'software developer' still raised eyebrows.
developer_1980_single: Even a good salary bent under the rate spike. Developers weren't immune.
developer_1980_dual: Two incomes in 1980 made most cities workable. The tech boom hadn't started yet.
developer_2000_single: Post-bubble, developers still had the map mostly green. The best decade for the trade.
developer_2000_dual: Two tech salaries in 2000 — comfortable almost everywhere. Peak middle-class moment.
developer_2020_single: Good salary, insane market. SF is red. Everywhere else? Still mostly fine.
developer_2020_dual: Two developers walk into a housing market. They do okay — except in San Francisco.
lawyer_1960_single: Top of the income ladder in 1960 meant top of the housing market too. No surprises.
lawyer_1960_dual: Two high incomes in 1960. The map is green, the martinis are dry.
lawyer_1980_single: Even lawyers felt the rate spike — stretched in a few cities, but mostly held.
lawyer_1980_dual: A partner's income on top of a lawyer's salary. Comfortable almost everywhere.
lawyer_2000_single: A lawyer in 2000 could afford most cities. SF was already testing the limits.
lawyer_2000_dual: Two professional incomes, one comfortable map. The upper-middle class still had options.
lawyer_2020_single: High earner, high market. Most cities are fine — San Francisco remains its own universe.
lawyer_2020_dual: Two incomes at this level and the map turns green. Even SF becomes a stretch, not a wall.
dentist_1960_single: At the top in 1960 meant buying whatever, wherever. The market had not yet lost its mind.
dentist_1960_dual: Two dentist salaries in 1960 is almost unfair. Green map, good life.
dentist_1980_single: The rate spike stung even at the top — a stretch in a few cities, comfortable in most.
dentist_1980_dual: Extra income at this level is overkill. Comfortable everywhere that matters.
dentist_2000_single: A dentist in 2000 could buy in almost any city. SF was already the exception.
dentist_2000_dual: Two high incomes — comfortable coast to coast. Even San Francisco blinked.
dentist_2020_single: The top earner in our model — and still a stretch in San Francisco. That's the story.
dentist_2020_dual: Two dentists, one American dream. Comfortable everywhere — yes, including SF.
